intro:
Cogitations appears in the King James Version of Daniel 7:28 as the translation of the Aramaic ra'yonay, meaning thoughts or musings. Daniel uses the term to describe the troubled inner reflections that followed his vision of the four great beasts and the Ancient of Days — the vision disturbed him greatly and his thoughts alarmed him, though he kept the matter to himself. The word thus captures the involuntary, tumultuous character of the mental activity provoked by a divine revelation whose significance exceeded the prophet's full comprehension.

The term is relatively rare in English Bible translation. Its use in Daniel underscores a recurring feature of prophetic experience: visionary revelation was not merely informative but deeply unsettling, pressing upon the mind and spirit of the recipient in ways that demanded time, prayer, and interpretation before clarity came. Daniel's cogitations in this passage parallel his troubled state after earlier visions (Daniel 7:15), marking the emotional and intellectual cost of receiving apocalyptic revelation. The Aramaic term belongs to a wider vocabulary of the inner life used throughout the Danielic visions.
